   One-liner: A CLI tool that auto-detects your hardware and ranks local LLMs by real, recency-aware benchmarks вҖ” not just parameter count.
   Why worth watching: With 3,800 stars and a 24-hour delta of +630, whichllm is experiencing a sustained surge in attention. The project has a recent release (within 30 days) and active commits within the last 7 days, indicating ongoing development and community engagement. The acceleration ratio is low (1.0), suggesting steady organic growth rather than a sudden spike.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward tooling that abstracts away the complexity of local LLM deployment. Developers should expect more 'auto-pilot' tools that handle hardware detection, model selection, and optimization, reducing friction for end users and enabling broader adoption of local AI.
   Also on: GitHub
   Problem solved: Choosing the best local LLM for your specific hardware is tedious and error-prone. whichllm automates hardware detection, VRAM estimation, and benchmark-aware ranking so you get the best model that actually runs on your machine with one command.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger вҖ” the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The recent release and continuous commits likely maintain momentum, and the tool addresses a clear pain point for the growing local LLM community.
   Target users: AI developers, ML engineers, hobbyists running local LLMs, and anyone evaluating hardware for local inference (e.g., planning a GPU purchase).
   Risk notes: The tool relies on HuggingFace API and external benchmark sources; if those change or become unavailable, functionality may degrade. The scoring system uses heuristics and confidence levels that may not always reflect real-world performance. Users should verify critical decisions with their own benchmarks.
   LLM confidence: high

Model & Demo Signals
1. OmniGameArena: A Unified UE5 Benchmark for VLM Game Agents with Improvement Dynamics
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09826v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.CV
   Summary: Vision-language model (VLM) agents are increasingly deployed in interactive game environments. Yet game benchmarks for VLM agents typically report a single first-attempt score per (agent, game) pair, focus on single-agent Solo play, and lack unified protocols for evaluating heterogeneous agent classes (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, and specialized game policies) on the same footing. 4. Rethinking the Divergence Regularization in LLM RL
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09821v1

   Summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key component of post-training large language models (LLMs). In practice, LLM RL is often off-policy because of training-inference mismatch and policy staleness, making trust-region control essential for stable optimization. Mainstream methods such as PPO and GRPO approximate this control with a ratio-clipping mechanism, but the importance ratio can be a poor proxy for distributional shift in long-tailed vocabularies. Recent work such as DPPO addresses this mismatch by replacing ratio-based clipping with a divergence-based mask, yielding a trust region defined by the sampled token's absolute probability shift. However, DPPO still relies on a hard mask: once a token crosses the trust-region boundary in a harmful direction, its gradient is discarded rather than corrected. To address this, we propose Divergence Regularized Policy Optimization (DRPO), which replaces the hard mask with a smooth advantage-weighted quadratic regularizer on policy shift. DRPO preserves the same trust-region geometry as DPPO while inducing bounded, continuous gradient weights that attenuate diverging updates and provide corrective signals beyond the boundary. Experiments across model scales, architectures, and precision settings show that DRPO improves the stability and efficiency of LLM RL training.

5. PTL-Diffusion: Manifold-Aware Diffusion with Periodic Terminal Laws
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.09816v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.CV
   Summary: Standard diffusion models typically use a single time-homogeneous Gaussian terminal distribution as the reference law for generation. While this choice is analytically convenient and empirically powerful, it provides little explicit structure for data concentrated near low-dimensional manifolds, where different regions of the data distribution may correspond to distinct local geometric or semantic factors. As a result, the reverse model must recover manifold-level structure almost entirely from an unstructured terminal reference distribution. We propose PTL-Diffusion, a proof-of-concept diffusion framework whose forward noising process converges to a nonconstant periodic family of Gaussian terminal laws rather than to a single invariant law. Unlike a phase-conditioned DDPM, where phase information only enters the denoising network while the forward process remains unchanged, PTL-Diffusion embeds phase structure directly into the forward noising dynamics. The proposed construction remains close to standard denoising diffusion models: for a periodically forced Ornstein--Uhlenbeck-type forward process, we derive closed-form forward marginals, the limiting periodic Gaussian terminal family, and explicit Gaussian reverse posteriors, enabling standard noise-prediction training. We also introduce an invariant-average regularization term coupling the phase-conditioned reverse dynamics through the averaged periodic reference law. Experiments on torus and cylinder point-cloud benchmarks and the Olivetti face dataset show that PTL-Diffusion improves manifold-level distributional matching over matched DDPM baselines, reducing phase-conditioned errors, feature-space covariance errors, and nearest-neighbour manifold distances. These results suggest structured terminal reference laws as a promising direction, while motivating more expressive phase constructions and larger-scale evaluations.
